question.text: In an options contract, which party has the right but not the obligation?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The option buyer/holder has the right but no obligation — they will exercise only when favorable. The option writer/seller has the obligation (legally bound to honor the contract) when the buyer exercises. For this right, the buyer pays a premium to the writer. **The Trap:** Both parties don't have rights — the asymmetry between buyer (right) and seller (obligation) is the defining feature of options. **Cross-Exam Context:** Option buyer/writer rights and obligations are fundamental to VIII, I, and X-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Option buying limits risk to the premium paid; option writing has theoretically unlimited risk — ensure clients understand this asymmetry before entering option positions.
